California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
July 16, 1991
Monte widders
Myers, widders & Gibson
5425 Everglades Street, Suite 100
Ventura, cA 93003
Re: Your Request for Infornal Assistance
our File No. I-90-212
Dear l,tr. widders:
You have requested advice on behalf of staal, Gardner &
Dunne, Inc. therdinafter referred to as "sG&D"), concerning
application of the conf l ict-of - interest provisions of .the.
piiiticar 
Reforn Act (the "Act").1 The following advice is based
upon the facts provided in your letter and the agreenent between
tire city of sini va11ey and sG&D, which you provided to tne on
June 1.7 , 1991 .
our advice is linited only to provisions of the Act. 
we
cannot provide advice about other conf t ict-of- interest laws, such
as coveinment code Section 1090. Since this request is general in
nature, we are treating your questions as a request for infornal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
OUESTIONS
1. Is sc&D, the contract geotechnical engineering firn to
the city, deened a consultant for purposes of the disclosure and
disqual i f ication provisions of the Act?
2. Are the employees of SG&D deened consuLtants for purposes
of the disclosure and disqual if ication provisions of the Act?
3. If the sG&D enployees are deemed to be consultants, what
obligations do they incur under the Act?
1 Go.r"arrr"nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the covernnent Code unless otherwi.se indicated. 
Comrnission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 
2
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramenro CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) tZ2-566O


File No. 
■-90-2■2
Page 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. A consul.tant must be a natural person and cannot be a
corporation. Consequently, SG&D is not a rrconsultantri for
purposes of the Act.
2. The enployees of SG&D vrho work on the city,s projects nay
be consultants if they provide infornation, advice, recommendation
or counsel to the city. 
However, if the enployees function
independently of the city's controL and direction, and possess no
authority rrith respect to any city decision beyond the rendition
of inforroation, advice, reconmendation or counsel, they rrill not
be consultants under the Act. I{e do not have sufficient
infornation about the relationship between the ci.ty and the
ernployees of SG&D. However, it appears that the employees nay be
consultants due to the long-standing contractual reJ,ationship
between sc&D and the city. 
The engineers review reports and
render advice on a regular basis; their opinions and analyses have
becone an integral part of the developnent permit revies process.
ff the city relies upon an engineer's expertise to approve or deny
a pernit or proposed project, the engineer is considered a
consuLtant.
3. Assurning the employees of sG&D are consultants under the
Act, they are requj.red to file statenents of econonic interests
pursuant to their designation in the city's conflict of interest
code. They are also required to disgualify fron participating in
any governnental. decision in which they have a financial interest.
FACPS
SG&D is a California corporation, consisting of geotechnical
engineers and geologists, which has been retained as consulting
geotechnical engineers by the city of sirni valley (the 'rcityrt)
since March 5, 1985. As contract geotechnical engineers, SG&D
specifically 'rreviews geotechnical reports and studies in
accordance with the city,s guidelines,r 
The reports are prepared
by other consultants as part of the developrnent permit review
process .
SG&D is asked to provide independent opinions regarding these
geotechnical reports. 
However, SG&D has no specific discretion or
decision-naking role or authority with respecl to the approval or
99!iul of the. proposed. proj ect itself. 
The opinions re-niered by
sc&D to the city are given without advice, diiection or contror of
the city staff. 
on the other hand, the independent contractual
relationship with the city is long-standing lnd SG&D is called
upon to render its independent advice on a regrular basis.
ANALYSIS
_ . Sectj,on. 8710o _prohibits, any.public official 
fron making,
participate in naking, or otherrrile using tris oi ir"i .iii.i;f


Flle NO. I-90-2■
2
Page 3
position to influence a governnental decision in which the
officiat 
has a financial interest. 
"Public officialrr is defined
in Section 82048 to include every uenber, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency-
Regulation 18700 (copy enclosed) defines public official 
and
consultant as fol lows:
(a) I'PubIic official at any leve1 of
state or local governnent' means every natural
person who is a nember, officer, eBployee or
consultant of a state or local government
agency .
***
(2) ttconsultant" shaIl include any
natural person who provides, under contract,
inforrnation, advice, recomnendation or counsel
to a state or local governnent agency,
provided, however, that I'consultant shall not
include a person who:
(A) conducts research and arrives
at conclusions with resPect to his or her
rendition of infornation, advice,
recomnendation or counsel independent of
the control and direction of the agency
or of any agency official, 
other than
normal contract rnonitoring; and
(B) Possesses no authoritY with
respect to any agency decision beyond the
rendition of infornation, advice,
recoNnendation or counsel.
Thus, according to Regulation 18700(a) (2), a consultant must
be a natural person and cannot be a corporation. Consequently,
SG&D, a corporation, is not a consultant for purposes of the Act.
However, the employees of SG&D who work on city projects nay
be consultants, and consequently, public officials, 
under the Act.
The employees are considered consultants if they provide
information, advice, recommendation or counsel to the city, are
subject to the city's control and direction, or possess authority
with respect to any city decisions. 
(Havden Advice Letter, No. A-
84-319, copy enclosed.) Conversely, if the employees act
independent of the city,s control and direction, and possess no
authority with respect to any city decision beyond the rendition
of information, advice, recommendation or counsel, the employees
are not considered consultants. (Regulation 18700(a) (2); Havden
Advice Letter, supra; Rose Advice Letter, No. A-84-306,. and Kaplan
Advice Letter, No. A-82-1o8, copies enclosed. )


Flle NO. I-90-212
Page 4
According to the facts provided, the SG&D firn has had an
independent contractual relationship with the city for over six
years and the engineers have been providing information, advice,
recommendation or counsel to the city on a reguJ,ar basis. This
long-standing relationship Eay provide regular contact with city
staff and officials 
as the engineers render ongoing advice. By
rendering ongoing advice, the sG&D engineers have become, in
essence, an extension of the city staff and their opinions and
advice are probably relied upon during the developnent perluit
review process. (See Criss Advice Letter, No. A-82-029.) If an
engineei provides advice or counsel or recontnends a specific
course of action on a continuing basis, he or she is deened to be
a consultant under the Act.
we do not have conpJ.ete infonnation about the relationship
between the city and the employees of SG&D. Hovrever, it appears
that the engineers rnay be consultants' An engineer who is deemed
a consultant is subject to both the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act. The engineer nust be
designated in the city's conflict of interest code under a
consultant category. As ve previously discussed, since the city's
conf lj.ct of interest code does not have a consultant category, lre
will suggest that the city amend its code.
Participate in Uakinq a Governmental Decision
Pursuant to Regulation 18700(c), a public official
Or
consultant 'rparticipates in the naking of a governrnental
when, acting within the authority of his or her position,
she :
(1) Negotiates, uithout significant substan-
tive revier.r, with a governtnental entity or private
person regarding the decision; or
(21 Advises or makes reconruendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:
(A) Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of
judgrnent on the part of the official or
designated enployee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, oraI1y or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the
part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.
decision':
he or
_ Presumably, the engineer will be providing infornation,
advice, reconroendation or counsel to the city ;ith respect i,o


File No. I-90-2■
2
Page 5
geotechnical reports prepared by other consultants. These reports
are utilized during the permit review process for private
developraent projects. 
If there is 'rsignificant intervening
substantive reviewrr of the engineerrs recornrnendations, the
engineer will not be participating in a governlaental decision.
(Regulation 18700(c); Leidiqh Advice Letter, No, A-89-320, copy
enclosed. )
However, the coEEission has interpreted this exception very
narrowly. In Kaplan, supra, we stated that:
A consultant participates in a decision, even
if it is reviewed by several of his superiors, if
those superiors rely on the data or analysis
prepared by the consultant nithout checking it
independently, if they rely on the professional
judgrment of the consultant, or if the consultant in
some other way actually nay influence the final
decision.
You have not provided us with sufficient infornation about
the relationship betseen the engineers and the city staff to
deternine if there is significant intervening substantive review.
However, under our interpretation, if the city staff Dakes a
decision on a perrnit or project and relies upon the engineer,s
infornation or expertise, the engineer is deerned to be
participating in a governnental decision.
Disoua 1i fication
An engineer who is a deemed to be a consultant under the Act
is a public officiat 
and is subject to the provisions of the Act.
The Act prohibits a public official 
fron naking, participating in,
or using his or her official 
position to influence a gov-rnurental
decision in which he or she knows or has reason to know of a
financial interest. 
(Section 8z1OO.) A public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a naterial financial efiect,
distinguishable froru its effect on the public Aenerally, on the
official 
or a menber of his irnrnediate firnily oi on:
(") 
Any business entity in r,rhich the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investrnent worth
one thousand dollars (S10O0) or nore.
. (b) Any reaL property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest r.rorth
one thousand dollars ($1O0O) or Dore.
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a connercial lendin{
institution in the regular course of busineis on
terns available to the public without regard to


File No. 
■-90-2■2
Page 6
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
doll.ars (S25o) or Dore in value provided to,
received by or pronised to the Public official
within 12 nonths prior to the tine rrhen the
decision is rnade.
(d) Any business
official 
is a director,
employee, or holds any
entity in which the public
officer, partner, trustee,
position of nanagenent.
(e) Any donor of, or any interBediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or nore in value
provided to, received by, or prornised to the public
ofticiat within 12 nonths prior to the time when
the decision is rnade.
Section 87103 (a) - (e) .
As a public official, 
the engineer nay not participate in a
decision :.i it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision wiIl
have a material financial effect on any of his or her econornic
interests. 
(Section 87103(a)-(e).) This is a separate analysis
rrhich can be done on a case-by-case basis when the appropriate
facts are provided.
I trust this answers your questions. If you have any further
questions regarding this natter, please contact ne at (916)
322-590L.
sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel
By:

Enc■osures
Division
. i Stecher
